coredata.dc:title: A hybrid imprinted polymer based on magnetic graphene oxide and carbon dots for ultrasonic assisted dispersive solid-phase microextraction of oxycodone
coredata.dc:creator: Ansari, Saeedeh
Masoum, Saeed
coredata.dc:description: To promote the development of molecular imprinting technique in the separation and analysis of oxycodone, molecularly imprinted polymer based on the magnetic graphene oxide and carbon dots nanoparticles (MIP@MGO/CDs NPs) was developed using ultrasonic-assisted dispersive solid-phase microextraction (UA-DSPME) and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). The morphology and structure characterization of synthesized nanoparticles are assessed by scanning electron microscope (SEM), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller analysis (BET), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). The adsorption isotherms of MIPs followed the Langmuir model with the maximum adsorption capacity of 99 mg/g and imprinting factor of 3.39. Under optimized conditions, which achieved by central composite design under response surface methodology, the detection limit (S/N = 3) was obtained 0.80 ng/mL in the linear range of 1–2000 ng/mL. The average recoveries of oxycodone were calculated from 92.50 to 103.20% in human urine samples with RSD less than 3.65%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dispersive solid-phase microextraction
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Magnetic separation
Carbon dots
Ultrasonic
Oxycodone

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs
Characterization of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs and NIP@MGO/CDs NPs
Experimental design and optimization of extraction condition
Preparation of samples and extraction procedure of OXY from human urine samples
UA-DSPME of OXY by MIP@MGO/CDs NPs
Synthesis of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs and NIP@MGO/CDs NPs
Optimization approach and analysis of variance
Linearity, limit of detection and limit of quantification

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the University of Kashan for supporting this work by Grant NO. 890412/2 and Parand Darou Pharmaceutical Chemicals Inc. (Saveh, Iran) and Mr. Majid Karimi for their supports.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic representation of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs preparation; (a) preparation of MGO/CDs; (b) polymerization of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs; (c) adsorption and desorption process for determination of OXY based on MIP@MGO/CDs NPs by UA-DSPMA and HPLC.
SEM images of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs (a) and NIP@MGO/CDs NPs (b); FTIR spectra of oxycodone, MGO/CDs, MIP-before washing, MIP-after washing, and NIP@MGO/CDs NPs (c).
Response surfaces for the central composite design: (a) eluent volume (mL)-adsorbent mass (mg); (b) adsorbent mass (mg)-sonication time (min); (c) adsorbent mass (mg)-pH.

(a) Adsorption isotherms of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs and NIP@MGO/CDs NPs; (b) Langmuir isotherm; (c) Freundlich isotherm; (d) Sips isotherm; (e) time profile; (f) Pseudo-first-order kinetics; (g) pseudo-second-order kinetics; (h) Reusability of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs.
Chromatograms of the biological samples, from the bottom to the top: (a) blank, (b) spiked (100 ng/mL), and (c) the spiked urine samples after desorption process.
Experimental design and results of central composite design based on the four-significant factors and the recoveries as the response.
Analysis of variance for reduced quadratic model.
Adsorption isotherm and kinetic isotherm parameters for OXY adsorption; imprinting factor (α) and selectivity factor (β) of the MIP@MGO/CDs NPs and NIP@MGO/CDs NPs.
Recoveries, intra-day and inter-day precision and accuracy obtained from determination of OXY at different spiked human urine samples by HPLC.
Analytical performances comparison of the proposed method based on the MIP@MGO/CDs NPs (present work) with other methods for determination of OXY.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: UHPLC-MS/MS, ultra-high-performance liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry; LLE, liquid–liquid extraction; HPLC–ESI-MS-MS, high-performance liquid chromatography– electrospray ionization-tandem mass spectrometry; LC–MS/MS, liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry; HPLC–HESI-MS-MS, high-performance liquid chromatography–heated assisted electrospray ionization-tandem mass spectrometry.

head.ce:title: A hybrid imprinted polymer based on magnetic graphene oxide and carbon dots for ultrasonic assisted dispersive solid-phase microextraction of oxycodone

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Kashan, Kashan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Kashan, Kashan, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To promote the development of molecular imprinting technique in the separation and analysis of oxycodone, molecularly imprinted polymer based on the magnetic graphene oxide and carbon dots nanoparticles (MIP@MGO/CDs NPs) was developed using ultrasonic-assisted dispersive solid-phase microextraction (UA-DSPME) and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). The morphology and structure characterization of synthesized nanoparticles are assessed by scanning electron microscope (SEM), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller analysis (BET), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). The adsorption isotherms of MIPs followed the Langmuir model with the maximum adsorption capacity of 99 mg/g and imprinting factor of 3.39. Under optimized conditions, which achieved by central composite design under response surface methodology, the detection limit (S/N = 3) was obtained 0.80 ng/mL in the linear range of 1–2000 ng/mL. The average recoveries of oxycodone were calculated from 92.50 to 103.20% in human urine samples with RSD less than 3.65%.

ce:section.ce:para: Opioid therapy is commonly used in treating postsurgical acute pain involving cancer-related pain, post-surgical pain, and chronic pain [1,2]. Oxycodone (14-hydroxy-7,8dihydrocodeinone, Fig. S1, OXY) as an opioid narcotic analgesic (extensively metabolize in the liver) is derived from opioid alkaloid thebaine [3], and has been used for treatment of moderate to severe pain [4]. Due to the powerful narcotic analgesic and highly addictive properties of OXY, concerns about addiction, toxicity, and efficacy have been raised, which caused to the main limitations of its clinical use. Therefore, achieving appropriate diagnostic tools in identifying and screening OXY can reduce these limitations.

To date, the analysis and determination of OXY and/or its metabolites in several published reports have been focused on high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5–8], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [9–20], various LC-based methods [21,22] and UV spectrophotometric technique [23]. In spite of the good advantages (due to their exact nature), most of these methods are economically costly, rigid experimental conditions, and complex steps that require qualified personnel.

Various microextraction methods are promising techniques for sample pretreatment of sensitive anticancer drugs. Meanwhile, dispersive solid-phase microextraction using ultrasonic-assisted techniques (UA-DSPME) possesses obvious advantages of less solvent consumption, low-cost, flexibility, rapidness, and simplified sample preparation. One of the most important advantages of this method is the reduction of extraction time by increasing the contact area between the adsorbent and the target molecules [24,25].

Molecular imprinting technique (MIT) is utilized as an efficient method for separation and purification. It is carried out by using an artificial polymer that contains complementary action sites and spatial configuration with a specific target molecule. The polymer materials made by this technology are called molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs). Due to their ease of synthesis, specific and selective recognition ability, good reusability, remarkable chemical/mechanical/thermal stability in acidic and basic conditions, and low cost, MIPs have been vividly described to generate “artificial locks” to match “molecular keys” in many extraction platforms [26,27]. Magnetic MIPs such as magnetic graphene oxide (MGO) composites, coated with a non-magnetic organic polymer, are materials that have both good magnetic characteristics and high adsorption/selectivity capacity. In relation to the conventional SPME, due to the direct contact between the sample and the solid phase, they have good properties such as reduction of analysis time, no need to cartridge packing, improvement of adsorption capacity, and less solvent quantity [28,29].

Graphene oxide (GO) particles with multiple oxygen moieties, hydrophilicity, and controllable electronic properties, are good support material that by large specific surface area, improve sensitivity and selectivity of MIPs [30]. In addition, graphene oxide is widely used as sorbent support for preconcentration of samples and it has emerged as an attractive nanosorbent owing to its outstanding properties like large surface area, high adsorption capacity and medium chemical and thermal stability [31]. Carbon dots (CDs), as the newest member of carbon-based quantum dots (CQDs), have green and simple synthetic routes, good photo stability, excellent water solubility, and easy functionalization [32,33]. Therefore, the combination of GO and CDs can be used to improve sensitivity, selectivity, and anti-interference ability of MIP-based nanosensor. In addition, the composite of magnetic GO/CDs due to the low-toxicity, biocompatibility, and highly fluorescent properties have shown good potential for a wide range of biomedical and chemical applications. Thus, GO and CDs can offer large specific surface area, which can facilitate the adsorption mechanism. The magnetic properties of Fe3O4 also save extraction time of sample by adsorbent. While, GO due to its medium chemical and thermal stability is needy to physically or chemically modify for overcoming the limitations and enhance its mechanical resistance [31].

Up till now, the reports about screening, selective adsorption, simple extraction and quantification of oxycodone using bio friendly adsorbents are seldom seen. In this work, MIP-based magnetic GO/CDs nanoparticles (MIP@MGO/CDs NPs) are prepared by surface polymerization. The molar ratio study and characterization analysis are carried out to enhance the adsorption parameters and performance of the synthesized adsorbents. Also, optimization of the extraction conditions is performed to enhance the quality of extraction procedure by response surface methodology (RSM) using central composite design (CCD). The high magnetic response simplifies the separation process and facilitates quick separation. Meanwhile, the ability of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs to identify and selective extraction of oxycodone from human urine samples was systematically evaluated.

Oxycodone and codeine were obtained from Parand Pharmaceutical Chemicals Inc. (Saveh, Iran). Buprenorphine was acquired from Darou-pakhsh Pharmaceutical Co. (Tehran, Iran). Ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) (99%), 2,2′-azoisobutyronitrile (AIBN), and methacrylic acid (MAA) (99%) were supplied from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Graphite powder, Iron (II) chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2·6H2O), Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), citric acid, ethylene diamine, sulfuric acid, potassium permanganate, hydrogen peroxide, phosphoric acid, chloroform, acetic acid, methanol, ethanol, hydrochloric acid, acetonitrile, ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH, 25%), and sodium 1-heptanesulfonate were supplied from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). The chemical reagents used in the experiment are of analytical grade. All solutions were prepared utilizing deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs

ce:section.ce:para: For preparation of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs as the final adsorbent, CDs, GO, MGO, and MGO/CDs were prepared with some modifications according to the literature [34–37], which are described in detail in the Electronic Supporting Material.

MIP@MGO/CDs were synthesized as reported in the literature with some modifications [38]. Functional monomer (MAA, 2.2 mmol), template molecules (oxycodone, 0.55 mmol), and polymerization solvent (chloroform, 30 mL) were firstly stirred to form initial complex for 12 h. Then, previously synthesized particles (MGO/CDs, 200 mg), cross-linker (EGDMA, 11 mmol), and initiator (AIBN, 50 mg) were added to the mixture. The resulted mixture was consequently stirred for 5 min (under vacuum) to remove dissolved oxygen and for 24 h heated at 60 ℃. The resulted MIP@MGO/CDs nanoparticles were subsequently rinsed with acetonitrile, diethyl ether, and ethanol. Finally, to remove the template molecules from the adsorbent, the resulted powder was washed using methanol/acetic acid (90:10 v/v) in a soxhlet extraction system, and dried in the air. The non-imprinted nanoparticles were synthesized without oxycodone as the template molecule.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs and NIP@MGO/CDs NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs and NIP@MGO/CDs NPs were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, AIS2100, Seron Technology, Korea). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) analyses were applied to evaluate average pore diameters, surface area, and pore volume of the obtained NPs by BELSORP-maxⅡ analyzer (MicrotracBEL, Japan) with nitrogen sorption porosimetry and a cylindrical pore model. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy using FT/IR-4100 spectrometer (Jasco, Japan) was used for identification of functional groups in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 at the room temperature and without any previous sample preparation. The magnetic properties of the produced Fe3O4/GO (MGO), MGO/CDs, and MIP@MGO/CDs NPs were analyzed using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) analysis at the room temperature (Lake Shore 7307, USA). A Linseis STA PT 1600 thermal analysis was used to thermogravimetry analysis (TGA), which the atmosphere condition, temperature range, and heating rate were nitrogen with the flow rate of 50 mL min−1, 25–800 °C, and 10 °C min−1, respectively. UV–Vis absorption measurements of the polymeric adsorbents were recorded on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer model UV-6300PC (VWR, Japan) at 230 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental design and optimization of extraction condition
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the relationships between influencing variables and response, the experiments for OXY adsorption were designed by RSM and CCD with four factors, including eluent volume (A, mL), adsorbent mass (B, mg), ultrasonic time (C, min), and pH (D) at five levels with 30 runs as presented in Table 1 . Design Expert 7.0.b1.1 (Stat-Ease, Inc., Minneapolis, USA) was used to all statistical analysis.

To evaluate the adsorption capacity of polymeric NPs, 30 mg of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs or NIP@MGO/CDs NPs were added into 10 mL of OXY solutions at different initial concentrations (10 to 500 μg/mL) prepared in 1 percent V/V solution of dilute acetic acid/water in 25 mL glass conical flasks. After that, to reach adsorption equilibrium, the mixtures were shaken using ultrasound-assisted process following immersion in an ultrasonic bath for 10 min at the room temperature; finally, after 5 min adsorption time, the supernatant and the magnetic NPs were separated with the help of an external magnetic field. The concentration of OXY in the supernatants was determined at 230 nm using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The equilibrium amount of OXY binding (Qe, mg/g) to MIP@MGO/CDs NPs or NIP@MGO/CDs NPs was calculated by the following equation [24].
(1)
Q e
=
(
C 0
-
C e
)
×
V m
where C0 (µg/mL) and Ce (µg/mL) are the initial and equilibrium concentration of OXY, respectively; m (mg) is the weight of the adsorbent and V (mL) is the initial volume of adsorption solution. All tests were conducted in triplicate.

In order to further investigate the adsorption capacity of the obtained polymers, 30 mg of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs or NIP@MGO/CDs NPs were added into 10 mL of 200 μg/mL OXY solutions prepared in 1 percent V/V solution of dilute acetic acid/water in 25 mL glass conical flasks based on the RSM results. The experimental solutions were prepared as described in section 2.4.1 and shaken for 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 min at the room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of samples and extraction procedure of OXY from human urine samples

ce:section.ce:para: Standard stock solutions of OXY (1.0 mg/mL) were prepared using dissolving 100 mg of OXY in 100 mL of 1 percent V/V solution of dilute acetic acid/water by sonicating for about 2 min. Human urine samples were collected from healthy volunteer (female, 25 years old) and centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 5 min; then, the supernatants were used for further investigations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UA-DSPME of OXY by MIP@MGO/CDs NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 30 mg of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs that previously soaked in 10 mL of the 1 percent V/V solution of dilute acetic acid/water, to remove any possible interference, poured into a glass vial. Then, the rinsed nanoparticles added into the samples; in the next step, ultrasound-assisted adsorption following immersion in an ultrasonic bath with the heating system of Tecno-GAZ SPA Ultra Sonic System at 40 kHz for 10 min at the room temperature was used to dispersion of the mixture. After 5 min relaxing, the adsorption process was performed and the separation process of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs containing OXY molecules was carried out by an external magnetic field; finally, the liquid phase was discarded.

In desorption process, MIP@MGO/CDs NPs dispersed in 15 mL of 90:10 (v/v) methanol/acetic acid as elution solvent to elute by ultrasonic bath, which was mentioned in full detail above. After 5 min desorption time, magnetic adsorbent separated by magnet and the desorbed solution with OXY analyzed to quantify the analyte concentration.

To illustrate the selectivity of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs, a standard solution of oxycodone, codeine, and buprenorphine (200 µg/mL, Fig. S1) was mixed with MIP@MGO/CDs or NIP@MGO/CDs NPs and the static adsorption (as described in section 2.4.1) was conducted to extraction procedure. Imprinting factor (α) and selectivity factor (β) were applied to measure the specific recognition ability of MIP@MGO/CDs or NIP@MGO/CDs NPs for OXY. The equations are as follows [24].
(2)
α =
Q
MIP
/
Q
NIP
(3)
β =
α 1
/
α 2
QMIP and QNIP are the amounts of OXY binding to MIP@MGO/CDs NPs and NIP@MGO/CDs NPs, respectively; α1 and α2 represent the imprinting factors of polymers to OXY and competitive agents, respectively.

From the point of cost view of effectiveness and stability, the sorbent reusability is a key feature. Accordingly, the reusability of 30 mg MIP@MGO/CDs NPs was studied using consecutive adsorption/desorption cycles; the adsorption/desorption procedures were entirely the same as described in Section 2.5.2.

Human urine samples with various concentration levels of OXY (10, 50, 100 ng/mL) were used to extract target molecules by UA-DSPME as extraction method, MIP@MGO/CDs NPs as synthetic adsorbent, and HPLC-UV as quantification technique. The adsorption/desorption procedures were entirely the same as described in Section 2.5.2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs and NIP@MGO/CDs NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis procedure of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs is depicted in Fig. 1 . MIP shell was constructed by OXY as template, MAA as functional monomer, and EGDMA as cross-linker with an optimized molar ratio of template to functional monomer to cross-linker of 1:4:20, which was coated on the MGO/CDs core. The surface imprinting material MIP@MGO/CDs NPs containing plenty of binding sites complementary to OXY in stereochemistry and functionality was prepared. The details of synthesize procedures and optimization roots are described in the Electronic Supporting Material.

The morphological structures of synthesized nanoparticles were shown in Fig. 2 a and 2b. The prepared NIPs or MIPs nanoparticles possessed good dispersibility. Additionally, both NIPs and MIPs had typical core/shell structure. The uneven and porous surface of the MIP@MGO/CDs NPs, which was attributed to the influence of the template molecules on the adsorbent morphology, facilitates the adsorption process of OXY from sample solutions. This result demonstrated that a typical core/shell structure, which the crosslinked polymer formed the shell layer to encapsulate the core of MGO/CDs NPs, could be fabricated in this method. The apparent core–shell structure could account for the rapid mass transfer process.

FTIR spectra for oxycodone, MGO/CDs, MIP-before washing NPs, leached MIP@MGO/CDs NPs, and NIP@MGO/CDs NPs were acquired (Fig. 2c). The two vibrational peaks at around 1153 cm−1 and 1220 cm−1 in FTIR spectrum of MGO/CDs are attributed to the formation of the either a bidentate or a monodentate complex between Fe and the carboxyl group on the surface of Fe3O4. The two characteristic absorption peaks from the Fe-O in Fe3O4 appear at 587 cm−1 and 632 cm−1. The broad absorption band at 3425 cm−1 is corresponded to the O–H stretching vibration; the two adsorption bands appear at 1625 cm−1 and 1725 cm−1 are attributed to the aromatic C = C vibration (resulted from skeletal vibration from unoxidized graphitic domains) and C = O stretching (from carboxyl groups and carbonyl), respectively. The absorption peak at 1151 cm−1 in FTIR spectrum of MIP-before washing is corresponded to the presence of oxycodone molecules before washing MIPs NPs. In FTIR spectra of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs and NIP@MGO/CDs NPs, the broad stretching vibration of O–H bond at 3425 cm−1 is assigned to the morphological and physical characterization of the MAA hydroxyl group. The two stretching vibration bonds at 1718 cm−1 and 2920 cm−1 are corresponded to C = O and C–H of methyl group, respectively. These results indicate the well polymerization condition.

The specific surface area and the pore volume, the saturation magnetization values, the crystalline structures and stabilities of synthesized nanoparticles were evaluated by BET, VSM, and TGA analyses. The details of these results are described in the Electronic Supporting Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization approach and analysis of variance
ce:section.ce:para: In order to determine the best microextraction condition, four variables (eluent volume, adsorbent mass, ultrasonic time, and pH) in five levels were determined by RSM based on CCD [39–41].

Due to the obtained lower P-value and higher F-value from the analysis of experimental results, a quadratic model with significant terms compare to other models suggested for this experimental design (Table S1). Analysis of variance (ANOVA) results that are related to the reduced quadratic model is shown in Table 2 . Generally, according to the results of Fisher's F-test, F-values and P-values were applied to check the significance of each coefficient and model, which smaller P-values and larger F-values indicated more significant terms of the model. As seen from the table, the high F-value of 67.71 and the low p-value (<0.0001) imply the suitability of the model. The F-value and P-value of “lack of fit” are 3.34 and 0.0944, respectively, which confirm the lack of fit is not significant.

According to CCD data analysis results, after removing of all insignificant terms, a quadratic equation was obtained as follows to calculate OXY adsorption efficiency:
(4) ER%=91.49 + 1.62A + 4.45B + 3.01C + 1.57D-1.04AD-0.88BD-1.30B2-0.51C2-3.01D2
where the terms of A, B, C and D are eluent volume, adsorbent mass, ultrasonic time, and pH, respectively. A higher value of R2 (0.968) illustrates a good correlation between the experimental data and polynomial regression model and can be desirable. Accordingly, the “Pred R2” and the “Adj R2” were 0.919 and 0.954, respectively, which is in reasonable agreement together. All of these results indicated that for predicting the OXY recovery, the reduced quadratic model is requisite and the experimental data was well fitted with the model.

Continuing the design of the experiments, to represent the variables effects on the experimental response, RSM was applied to evaluate the optimum conditions. The 3D plots of experimental response based on the model equation are shown in Fig. 3 . The variables were eluent volume, adsorbent mass, ultrasonic time, and pH, which the goal of the optimization process was to achieve the highest performance (recovery) by RSM.

Adsorbent mass is one of the important parameters in the adsorption and microextraction process of OXY. In fact, as shown in Fig. 3 (a, b and c), the experimental response increased with increase in adsorbent mass; however, by increasing of the adsorbent mass no significant improvement in the experimental response of OXY microextraction was occurred, which is attributed to saturation of active specific recognition sites on the synthesized adsorbent.

As shown in Fig. 3(b), the response (ER%) increases initially by the increase of ultrasonic time that can be due to the abundance of available specific recognition sites and no significant improvement is presented after equilibrium condition. This increase is related to the good dispersion of solid phase into the microextraction solution and increased surface area.

As shown in Fig. 3(c), the equilibrium extraction percentage of analyte decreased slowly with decreasing pH. It was found that due to the formation of the covalent bonds at the high pH values and their dissociation at the low pH values. The recognition sites of the coated polymers in the low pH values introduce hydrophilic form and swollen behavior.
According to the CCD results based on RSM design matrix, the optimized values of eluent volume, adsorbent mass, ultrasonic time, and pH were achieved at 15 mL, 30 mg, 10 min, 4.5, respectively.

The isothermal adsorption of OXY on MIP@MGO/CDs NPs and NIP@MGO/CDs NPs at the room temperature was displayed in Fig. 4 a. It was found that the adsorption capacity of OXY-MIP@MGO/CDs NPs increased rapidly with the increasing of initial concentration of OXY, and it almost came up to saturation at the concentration of 200 μg/mL and both of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs and NIP@MGO/CDs NPs tend to reach an adsorption plateau.

As can be seen from Fig. 4 (b, c and d), the data of the adsorption experiments fitted by corresponding linear forms of Langmuir (Ce/qe versus Ce), Freundlich (lnqe versus lnCe), and Sips models (qe versus Ce 1/n). In the Electronic Supporting Material, the original forms of these isotherms are given in detail [38].

The parameters of isothermal models are tabulated in Table 3 . It was observed that for MIP@MGO/CDs NPs and NIP@MGO/CDs NPs, Langmuir isotherm model with good correlation coefficient values (R2 = 0.997 and 0.993) fitted better than Freundlich model (R2 = 0.942 and 0.907) and Sips model (R2 = 0.997 and 0.983). From the slope and intercept of the Langmuir equation, the maximum adsorption capacity of OXY on MIP@MGO/CDs NPs at equilibrium was 99.00 mg/g, which was 3.4 times more than the amount on the NIP@MGO/CDs NPs (29.24 mg/g). These resulted capacities were close to the test results. Since one of the good features of graphene oxide and carbon dots, as mentioned before, is the large surface area and high adsorption capacity, so the adsorption capacity of the synthesized molecularly imprinted polymers can be compared with other synthesized polymers without graphene oxide and/or carbon dots. By doing this comparison, the difference in the adsorption capacity of adsorbents can be clearly seen [24,26]. It suggested that the spatial location and chemical effects of imprinted sites on the surface of MIPs were more appropriate for being the imprinted sites of template molecules and MIP@MGO/CDs NPs showed higher affinity to the target molecule than NIP@MGO/CDs NPs.

One of the most important features of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs in practical application is adsorption kinetics for the fast separation process. The pseudo-first-order model and pseudo-second-order models were carried out in order to investigate the rate-limiting step and adsorption mechanism, which are expressed in detail in the Electronic Supporting Material [24].

Fig. 4e demonstrates the Q-t curve of both MIPs and NIPs. The initial Q value increased with the increase of time. However, when the asorption time reached approximately 30 min, Q value attained the maximum; then, the variation of Q value tended to be gentle, finally achieving the adsorption equilibrium.

It was observed in Fig. 4(f, g) that the experimental data for MIP@MGO/CDs NPs and NIP@MGO/CDs NPs is fitted by second-order kinetics equation with the good correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.995 and 0.996) is more efficient than the first-order model (R2 = 0.970 and 0.980). The related parameters of Kinetic adsorption models are also shown in Table 3. It also suggested that the chemical course in the adsorption process, including sharing electrons between the nanosorbents and the template molecules could be the rate-limiting step.

To investigate the specific binding ability of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs for OXY, two structural analogues including Codeine and Buprenorphine were selected as competitors according to the various molecular weights. The specificity of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs and NIP@MGO/CDs NPs was assessed by imprinting factor (α) and selectivity factor (β). The determined values of α toward OXY (3.38) was greater than Codeine (2.49) and Buprenorphine (1.48). The adsorption properties of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs and NIP@MGO/CDs NPs for different molecules are listed in Table 3. Owing to the existence of binding sites matching the target OXY on the surface of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs, the adsorption amount and IF of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs for OXY were the highest, which means the template molecules have the higher affinity than other analogues.

The β values of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs for Codeine and Buprenorphine were 1.36 and 2.28, respectively (see Table 3). The above results indicated that the synthesized imprinted polymers can be specifically rebinding the target OXY.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linearity, limit of detection and limit of quantification
ce:section.ce:para: The linearity, limits of detection (LOD) and limits of quantification (LOQ) of the suggested method were investigated for OXY. The calibration curve based on three determinations of standard solutions at nine concentration levels was obtained at the optimal conditions for OXY. It showed a good linearity in the range of 1–2000 ng/mL and the regression equation of A = 0.0199C + 1.4386 with the correlation coefficient of 0.998 (Fig. S2). The LOD and LOQ of the developed MSPE method were calculated at a signal to noise ratio (S/N) of 3 and 10, respectively. On this method, the LOD and LOQ values for OXY were 0.80 ng/mL and 2.67 ng/mL, respectively.

To assess the regeneration ability, method robustness and reusability of the MIP@MGO/CDs NPs and NIP@MGO/CDs NPs, the samples were adsorbed and desorbed for the same concentration using 12-repeated adsorption/desorption cycles, and the results are shown in Fig. 4h. After these repeated cycles of adsorption/desorption, the maximum adsorption capacity of MIPs remained the same (only 3.6% decrease compared to the initial adsorption capacity), implying that the obtained MIPs had good reusability and stability.

In this section, spiked human urine samples were utilized as a real sample to investigate the practical application performance of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs. Analysis of the spiked human urine samples revealed that the accuracy was 92.50–103.20% with precision of 1.25–3.65% (Table 4 ). Accordingly, for sensitive, selective and accurate recognition of OXY in biologic samples, UA-DSPME as extraction method and MIP@MGO/CDs NPs as adsorbent are applicable.

The corresponding HPLC chromatograms for the samples are shown in Fig. 5 . As can be seen from Fig. 5A, no chromatographic peaks related to OXY are observed. After spiking of template molecules to the raw sample, the chromatographic peak of template molecules is observed in Fig. 5B. In the chromatogram of samples after desorption process (Fig. 5C), the annoying peaks related to the impurities observed in previous chromatograms are no longer present, which proved the excellent selectivity properties of MIP@MGO/CDs NPs towards template molecules.

The application of microextraction/clean-up approach for the determination/quantification of OXY by nanoscale-based MIP has not been studied yet. The analytical results of the proposed method were compared with the results of other methods for oxycodone extraction. As seen from Table 5 , although these methods have good advantages (due to their exact nature), the proposed HPLC based method using MIP@MGO/CDs NPs has good competitive ability against them for trace determination of OXY in real samples. By using a simple magnet to collect the magnetic nanoparticles as adsorbent, the extraction procedures simplified and the required time decreased dramatically. Based on our knowledge, the homemade MIP@MGO/CDs NPs is the first try in application of MIP-based sorbent in detection and determination of OXY.

MIP-based adsorbent was smartly prepared using OXY as a template and magnetic GO/CDs. MIP@MGO/CDs NPs as adsorbent combined by UA-DSPME and HPLC, which was applied to the selective extraction of OXY from human urine samples. The prepared MIP@MGO/CDs NPs combine the advantages of magnetic GO/CDs nanoparticles, and surface imprinting. As a consequence, it exhibits several advantages including promising mechanical strength, high specific surface area, easy separation and mass transfer. The prepared MIP@MGO/CDs NPs under optimized UA-DSPME conditions provided fast kinetics and more binding capacity than those of traditional MIPs, which facilitated high detection sensitivity. Besides, the combination of CCD and RSM was applied as an experimental based design approach to optimize extraction conditions. It was proven that the combination of UA-DSPME method and the proposed MIP@MGO/CDs NPs based on HPLC-UV technique could provide a fast, cost-effective approach with wide linear ranges and low LOD for the analysis of real samples. Therefore, the development of this work provides a new, efficient and convenient nanomaterial for the identification and separation of OXY from complex biological samples.
Saeedeh Ansari: Investigation, Visualization, Methodology. Saeed Masoum: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the University of Kashan for supporting this work by Grant NO. 890412/2 and Parand Darou Pharmaceutical Chemicals Inc. (Saveh, Iran) and Mr. Majid Karimi for their supports.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.105988.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
